historical_range.description: This plot shows the relationship between daily water depth range (max to min) and the corresponding daily maximum water depth since the Flash Flood Observatory began operating. A clear upward trend is visible: days with larger water depth ranges also reach higher maximum water depths.
historical_range.empty_message: Daily range and peak-level context will appear here after the local analysis record is built.
historical_range.footer_description: Most days cluster at low values (bottom left), reflecting stable, non-event conditions, while a small number of events form a separate group (top right) with both high ranges and high maximum depths. This pattern supports the classification of Boscastle as a flashy catchment where steep slopes and slow-draining geology cause rapid response even from small, localised events.
historical_range.subtitle: Daily Range and Peak Levels

level_heatmap.description: This heatmap shows the maximum daily water depth as a percentage of the average water depth recorded since the Flash Flood Observatory deployment in November 2025 (0.263 m). Each cell represents a specific day of the week and month, allowing patterns in river behaviour to be seen across the Observatory's operation. The colours indicate how each day's maximum level compares to the average: days close to the average appear white (⬜), wetter-than-average days appear blue (🟦) or purple (🟪), and drier-than-average days appear brown (🟫).
level_heatmap.empty_message: Daily maximum river-level heatmap data will appear here after the historical record is built.
level_heatmap.footer_description: This makes it easy to identify periods of typical flow, sustained dry spells, and clusters of high-water days associated with storm events or rapid catchment response. A value such as 30% means the river reached 0.30 × the average (0.30 × 0.263 m = 0.0789 m, represented in brown 🟫), while 450% means 4.50 × the average (4.50 × 0.263 m = 1.1829 m, represented in purple 🟪).
level_heatmap.title: % of Flash Flood Observatory Average

response.description: River flow and rainfall over the last 24 hours, shown together on one chart. Rainfall totals (Environment Agency station 49149, 15-min in millimetres) are plotted on the left, while river flow rate (volume per time, m³/s) is calculated and plotted on the right. Date & Time are shown along the bottom.

descriptions.24h: River flow and rainfall over the last 24 hours, shown together on one chart. Rainfall totals (Environment Agency station 49149, 15-min in millimetres) are plotted on the left, while river flow rate (volume per time, m³/s) is calculated and plotted on the right. Date & Time are shown along the bottom.
descriptions.5d: River flow and rainfall over the last 5 days, shown together on one chart. Rainfall totals (Environment Agency station 49149, 15-min in millimetres) are plotted on the left, while river flow rate (volume per time, m³/s) is calculated and plotted on the right. Date & Time are shown along the bottom.

response.empty_message: River flow event analysis is temporarily unavailable.
response.footer_description: This allows correlation between rainfall and the change of river level. It also helps show the response time of the river to rain and the speed at which the volume of water rises.

response.subtitle: River Hydrograph and Precipitation Hyetograph

notes.label: Why Boscastle
notes.text: Flash floods are increasing with climate change. Boscastle is a flashy catchment. Steep slopes and slow draining geology cause rapid response, even from small, localised events.

notes.label: How it is observed
notes.text: We use innovative remote-sensing techniques (LiDAR, ultrasonic altimeter, camera) to monitor flash floods in real time, capturing accurate data on their speed and intensity.

notes.label: Impact
notes.text: Enhance early warning systems and flood mitigation strategies to better protect communities from flash floods. While the case study focuses on Boscastle, UK, the methodology is applicable to other locations.

official_alert.disclaimer: Official warning information relates to Environment Agency flood alerts and warnings.

official_alert.message: No Environment Agency flood warnings or alerts are currently active in Boscastle, Cornwall.

site.name: Flash Flood Observatory
site.strapline: This dashboard monitors flash floods in real time, capturing data on their speed and intensity. The Flash Flood Observatory is currently monitoring the River Valency in Boscastle, UK. Developed by researchers from the Universities of Greenwich, Bath, and TU Delft and funded by the Royal Society, it aims to improve early warning systems and flood mitigation.
